meta.title: Latest query loses to stale search results
meta.summary: Cached results and slower network results both update the same list, so an older query can briefly show up again.
signals: Results briefly jump back to an older query
Cached and network results both update the same results list
Debounce reduced traffic, but the stale flash is still there

context.symptom: A user types 'react', quickly deletes back to 're', and briefly sees old 'react' results again.
context.userImpact: Search feels unreliable because the interface does not reflect the most recent intent.
context.environment: Plain JavaScript search with a 250ms debounce, a small cache of recent queries, and one shared results list used by both cached and network results.

evidence.title: Request order
evidence.body: The request for 'react' finishes after the request for 're', and both update the same results list.

evidence.title: Current cache + response handling
Observed behavior
evidence.body: A 250ms debounce reduced the number of requests, but the stale flash still appears on a slow network when you backspace quickly.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.helperText: Look for why old results are still allowed to replace the current list.

options.label: The debounce window is too short, so the UI ends up showing the wrong query.
options.feedback: A longer debounce might reduce overlap, but it does not fix a shared update path that still lets old results replace the screen.

options.label: Cached results and network responses both write into the same shared list with no check for the active query, so older work can replace the screen again.
options.feedback: Correct. Cached and network data can both update the same list without checking whether the query is still current.

options.label: The browser should use Promise.race so the fastest response always wins.
options.feedback: Fastest is not the same as latest. This framing confuses a race primitive with product intent.

options.label: The main issue is just that the cache sometimes has old data for the query.
options.feedback: Old cache data can contribute, but the deeper bug is that multiple sources can still update the same visible list with no guard.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: You want to prove exactly where old results are still allowed to replace the visible list.

candidates.label: Reproduce on a slow network while logging the query text, whether data came from cache or network, and the request ids.
Check whether cached results and network responses both write into the same shared results list.
Confirm that the data being rendered belongs to an older query when the flash happens.
Rewrite the search endpoint to return smaller payloads first.
Increase debounce to 600ms before you understand the race.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes should be part of the first robust fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the changes that make every update respect the latest query.

options.label: Send cached results and network responses through one update function that first checks whether this is still the active query.
options.feedback: Correct. One guarded update path is safer than multiple blind writers.

options.label: Track a request id or query version and ignore responses that are no longer current.
options.feedback: Correct. A request id or query version can stop older responses from winning.

options.label: Wrap all requests in Promise.race so the fastest response updates the UI.
options.feedback: Fastest response is explicitly the wrong goal here. Latest intent is what matters.

options.label: Abort or supersede older in-flight requests when a newer query starts.
options.feedback: Correct. This reduces overlap, but the real guarantee still comes from checking before you update the UI.

options.label: Keep only the debounce; it already reduces overlap enough.
options.feedback: Debounce can help load, but alone it does not guarantee correct ordering.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best way to keep this from coming back?
stages.helperText: Pick the check that proves older results can no longer overwrite the latest query.

options.label: Reduce API latency budgets so requests are less likely to overlap.
options.feedback: Useful, but lower latency does not replace correctness guarantees in the client.

options.label: Cache every result forever so the client no longer needs to issue new searches.
options.feedback: That changes product behavior and still does not solve the stale update logic.

options.label: Do a quick manual smoke test before release and call it done.
options.feedback: Manual checks are helpful, but weak against timing-sensitive regressions.

options.label: Add an integration test with cached results plus delayed network responses, and assert that only the active query can update the list.
options.feedback: Correct. That covers the real messy flow here: multiple sources trying to write into one visible list.

scoreBands.label: You treated it like a traffic problem, not a correctness problem
scoreBands.summary: The real issue was who is allowed to update the visible list, not only how many requests were sent.

scoreBands.label: Mostly right, but one safety check is still missing
scoreBands.summary: You saw the race, but the debug order or guardrail did not fully lock it down.

scoreBands.label: You kept the latest query in charge
scoreBands.summary: You proved the stale flash, guarded the shared update path, and tested the exact cache-plus-network race.

idealRunbook: Reproduce the bug and log cached and network results together.
Prove that one shared update path accepts old data after a newer query.
Add guards so only the current query can update the list.
Protect the flow with a cache-plus-delayed-response test.

teachingBlocks.title: The easy trap
teachingBlocks.text: Once cached and network data both write into the same list, this is no longer just a 'too many requests' problem. It is also a 'who is allowed to update the UI' problem.

teachingBlocks.title: Stronger interview framing
items: Name it as an async race condition.
Point out that both sources write into the same list.
Explain why the latest query must always win.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If you keep the cache, what one rule must be true before anything is allowed to update the results list?
